clinicalStem: A 24-year-old woman presents after her first witnessed convulsive episode. Her boyfriend reports that before the main shaking started she turned her head to the right, her right arm stiffened, then the jerking spread to involve her whole body. She was confused for 20 minutes afterwards. She has no significant past medical history. Neurological examination is normal. Which is the MOST accurate description of this seizure type?
options: Generalised tonic-clonic seizure with no focal onset
Focal onset seizure with impaired awareness evolving to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure
Tonic seizure arising from diffuse cortical irritability
explanation: The key features are a clear focal onset (versive head turning, ipsilateral arm stiffening) before bilateral spread, followed by postictal confusion. This pattern is diagnostic of a focal onset seizure that evolves to a bilateral tonic-clonic seizure (previously called secondary generalised). Absence seizures do not present with focal motor signs or prolonged postictal confusion. True primary generalised seizures have no focal onset.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A primary generalised tonic-clonic seizure has no preceding focal features; the versive head turning and unilateral arm stiffening make this incorrect.
Absence seizures are brief (seconds), have no postictal phase, and do not present with unilateral motor signs before generalisation.
This is the correct classification: focal onset with evolution to bilateral tonic-clonic seizure.
Myoclonic-atonic seizures involve sudden loss of muscle tone following a myoclonic jerk; this presentation does not fit.
Tonic seizures involve sustained stiffening without the preceding focal versive and clonic phase described here.
clinicalPearl: Head and eye deviation to one side before a convulsion localises the seizure focus to the contralateral hemisphere and indicates focal (not primary generalised) epilepsy.
keyClue: Head turning to one side and unilateral arm stiffening before generalisation
commonExamTrap: Confusing secondary generalisation with primary generalised tonic-clonic seizure — the two are managed differently (focal epilepsy needs MRI and may require surgery).
missedConcept: Focal onset features (aura, automatisms, unilateral signs) must be actively sought in every seizure history.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male medical student presents with three episodes over the past two months of brief jerking of his arms on waking, causing him to drop his morning coffee. He has had one generalised convulsion. His neurological examination is entirely normal. Routine blood tests, glucose, and ECG are normal. An EEG is performed on a Tuesday morning after a sleep-deprived Monday night. What finding on EEG would BEST support a diagnosis of juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME)?
options: Focal slow-wave discharge over the left temporal region
3 Hz spike-and-slow-wave complexes provoked by hyperventilation
Generalised 4–6 Hz polyspike-and-wave discharges, accentuated on sleep deprivation
Unilateral temporal sharp waves during photic stimulation
explanation: JME is an idiopathic generalised epilepsy characterised by early-morning myoclonic jerks, occasional generalised tonic-clonic seizures, and sometimes absence seizures. Its hallmark EEG finding is generalised 4–6 Hz polyspike-and-slow-wave discharges, most prominent after sleep deprivation and on awakening — which is exactly why this EEG was performed after sleep deprivation. Sleep deprivation is the standard activation technique for JME.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Focal temporal slow waves suggest a structural focal epilepsy or encephalopathy, not JME which is a generalised syndrome.
3 Hz spike-and-wave provoked by hyperventilation is the classic EEG finding in childhood absence epilepsy, not JME.
Generalised 4–6 Hz polyspike-and-wave after sleep deprivation is the correct answer for JME.
Burst suppression is seen in severe encephalopathy, deep anaesthesia, or hypoxic-ischaemic injury — not in JME.
Unilateral temporal sharps during photic stimulation are inconsistent with JME; photoparoxysmal response in JME is generalised.
clinicalPearl: JME: morning myoclonus + generalised 4–6 Hz polyspike-wave on sleep-deprived EEG — responds well to valproate but relapse is common if stopped.
keyClue: Early-morning arm jerking causing dropped objects in a teenager/young adult
commonExamTrap: Mistaking JME for absence epilepsy because both are generalised; JME EEG shows faster (4–6 Hz) polyspike-wave, not the classic 3 Hz of absence.
missedConcept: Sleep deprivation is a powerful activating technique that unmasks generalised discharges in JME.

clinicalStem: A 35-year-old man with known epilepsy is brought to the emergency department by ambulance. He has been convulsing continuously for 32 minutes. He receives IV lorazepam 4 mg x2 without cessation of seizures. His temperature is 38.2°C, glucose 4.8 mmol/L, Na 136 mmol/L, K 3.8 mmol/L. He has no known drug allergies. Which is the MOST appropriate NEXT investigation to guide immediate management?
options: CT head without contrast to exclude structural cause
Lumbar puncture to exclude CNS infection
explanation: This is established status epilepticus refractory to two doses of benzodiazepine. Before escalating to second-line agents (e.g., levetiracetam, phenytoin, valproate), checking serum antiepileptic drug levels immediately guides management: sub-therapeutic levels of a patient's usual drug identify a correctable trigger and inform drug choice. CT head is important but does not alter the immediate pharmacological escalation plan as urgently as knowing drug levels. LP is deferred until seizures are controlled and raised ICP excluded.
wrongAnswerExplanations: EEG may be useful later to detect non-convulsive status but does not guide the immediate pharmacological step; clinical diagnosis of status is already established.
CT head is important to exclude structural causes but should not delay drug escalation; levels are faster and more immediately actionable.
Serum antiepileptic drug levels are the best next investigation — sub-therapeutic levels are the commonest preventable cause of breakthrough status.
Lumbar puncture is contraindicated until seizures are controlled and raised ICP has been excluded by imaging; it should not be the next step.
MRI is superior for epilepsy evaluation but is logistically impractical and time-consuming during active convulsive status.
clinicalPearl: In known epileptics presenting in status, sub-therapeutic drug levels are the most common reversible precipitant — check levels before escalating to second-line agents.
keyClue: Known epilepsy + 32 minutes of continuous convulsion + failed two doses of benzodiazepine
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to CT head before checking drug levels — imaging is important but does not alter the immediate pharmacological escalation.
missedConcept: Status epilepticus management follows a time-based protocol; investigation (drug levels, glucose, electrolytes) runs in parallel with treatment.
redFlagReason: Convulsive status epilepticus > 30 minutes carries high risk of neuronal injury, hypoxia, rhabdomyolysis, and death; immediate structured escalation is mandatory.

clinicalStem: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency department reporting that 6 hours ago she developed the 'worst headache of her life' that reached maximum intensity within seconds while she was gardening. She vomited twice. She has no neck stiffness on examination, GCS 15, BP 158/96 mmHg, and no focal neurological deficits. A non-contrast CT head performed 5 hours after headache onset is reported as normal. What is the MOST appropriate next investigation?
options: Repeat non-contrast CT head in 12 hours
Serum D-dimer to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis
explanation: A thunderclap headache reaching maximum intensity in seconds raises high suspicion for subarachnoid haemorrhage (SAH). Non-contrast CT is approximately 98% sensitive within the first 6 hours but sensitivity falls with time. When CT is negative, lumbar puncture at 12 hours post-onset (to allow haemoglobin to lyse to oxyhaemoglobin/xanthochromia) is the gold standard to detect SAH. Xanthochromia persists for up to 2 weeks. CT angiography follows if SAH is confirmed, not before LP.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI FLAIR can detect SAH but is not routinely used as the second-line investigation in the acute setting when LP is available; sensitivity at 6 hours is debated.
LP at 12 hours post-onset for xanthochromia is the correct gold-standard second step when CT is negative in suspected SAH.
CT angiography is used after SAH is confirmed to identify the aneurysm; it should not be the immediate next step before confirming blood.
Repeating CT head later adds radiation and delay without the diagnostic yield of LP; CT sensitivity for SAH drops further with time.
D-dimer is not a reliable or validated test for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis in this acute thunderclap presentation.
clinicalPearl: Normal CT head does NOT exclude SAH — LP for xanthochromia at 12 hours post-onset is mandatory when clinical suspicion is high.
keyClue: Thunderclap headache (maximum intensity within seconds) + normal CT at 5 hours
commonExamTrap: Stopping the workup after a 'normal' CT — CT misses up to 2% of SAH even in the first 6 hours and sensitivity drops thereafter.
missedConcept: The gold-standard investigation after negative CT in suspected SAH is LP for xanthochromia, not repeat CT or immediate angiography.
redFlagReason: Thunderclap headache must be assumed to be subarachnoid haemorrhage until proven otherwise; missed SAH carries mortality of up to 50%.

clinicalStem: A 28-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes over the past year. Each episode begins with zigzag shimmering lines expanding across her visual field over 20–25 minutes, then a severe unilateral throbbing headache lasting 6–10 hours, with nausea and photophobia. She takes the combined oral contraceptive pill. Neurological examination between attacks is normal. Which is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: Migraine with typical aura is diagnosed when visual aura (typically a scintillating scotoma — zigzag shimmering lines) spreads gradually over 20–30 minutes (the slow march distinguishes it from TIA) and is followed by headache. The ICHD criteria require at least one aura symptom spreading over 5 minutes, lasting 5–60 minutes, with associated headache. TIA visual symptoms are typically negative (monocular loss of vision) and last under 30 minutes without subsequent headache. The COC use is important as migraine with aura is a contraindication to combined oral contraceptives due to increased stroke risk.
wrongAnswerExplanations: TIA typically causes negative visual symptoms (monocular vision loss — amaurosis fugax) or sudden onset, not a 25-minute positive spreading scintillating scotoma followed by headache.
Migraine without aura lacks the preceding visual phenomenon; this patient clearly has an aura phase.
Migraine with typical aura is the correct diagnosis — the classic expanding scintillating scotoma followed by unilateral throbbing headache with nausea and photophobia.
Occipital epilepsy causes brief (seconds to minutes), brightly coloured circular visual hallucinations, not a gradually expanding scintillating scotoma lasting 25 minutes.
Retinal detachment causes a curtain-like loss of vision and floaters; it does not cause shimmering/scintillating scotomata or headache.
clinicalPearl: Migraine with aura is an absolute contraindication to the combined oral contraceptive pill because it doubles the already elevated stroke risk.
keyClue: Gradual 20–25 minute expansion of a scintillating visual scotoma followed by unilateral throbbing headache with nausea/photophobia
commonExamTrap: Diagnosing TIA because of visual symptoms — TIA visual phenomena are sudden, brief, and negative (loss of vision), not gradual positive spreading scintillations.
missedConcept: The slow spread (march) of aura over 5–30 minutes is the hallmark that distinguishes migraine aura from vascular events.

clinicalStem: A 26-year-old obese woman (BMI 37 kg/m²) presents with a 3-month history of daily headaches, worse on waking and with Valsalva manoeuvre, and transient visual obscurations lasting seconds. She is taking oral tetracycline for acne. Visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally. Fundoscopy reveals bilateral papilloedema. Neurological examination is otherwise normal. A CT head is reported as normal with no mass lesion. What is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement
explanation: This is a classic presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH): obese young woman, daily headache worse on waking, transient visual obscurations, bilateral papilloedema, normal CT, and tetracycline use (a known precipitant). The diagnosis requires demonstration of elevated CSF opening pressure (> 25 cmH2O in adults) on lumbar puncture after neuroimaging has excluded a mass lesion. LP is both diagnostic and can be therapeutic (removal of CSF reduces pressure). MRI/MRV should be performed before LP to exclude venous sinus thrombosis but LP is the confirmatory test.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Visual evoked potentials assess optic nerve conduction and are useful for demyelination but do not confirm elevated intracranial pressure.
MRI brain and MRV are important to exclude cerebral venous sinus thrombosis as a cause of raised ICP but do not measure pressure; they should precede LP, not replace it.
LP with opening pressure measurement is the gold-standard confirmatory investigation for IIH — an opening pressure > 25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition is diagnostic.
Formal perimetry monitors visual field loss (enlarged blind spot is the earliest change in IIH) and guides treatment but does not confirm the diagnosis.
Ambulatory blood pressure monitoring is useful for headache related to hypertension; BP is not described as abnormal and this does not explain bilateral papilloedema.
clinicalPearl: IIH diagnosis requires LP with opening pressure > 25 cmH2O after imaging excludes a mass; treat with acetazolamide and weight loss.
keyClue: Obese young woman + bilateral papilloedema + transient visual obscurations + tetracycline use + normal CT
commonExamTrap: Stopping at MRI/CT as the confirmatory test — imaging excludes secondary causes but only LP opening pressure confirms the elevated ICP.
missedConcept: LP opening pressure > 25 cmH2O (with the patient relaxed and legs extended) is the diagnostic criterion for IIH.

clinicalStem: A 68-year-old right-handed man is brought to the emergency department with sudden onset left-sided face and arm weakness and slurred speech. His wife reports his arm weakness is worse than his leg. He also has left-sided sensory loss and neglects the left side of the room. His NIHSS score is 14. BP 186/102 mmHg, AF on ECG, blood glucose 6.2 mmol/L. Which vascular territory does this stroke MOST likely involve?
explanation: The patient is right-handed (left hemisphere dominant). The deficits are contralateral to the lesion, so left-sided weakness and sensory loss localise to the right hemisphere. Within the right hemisphere, MCA territory supplies the face and arm more than the leg (the leg is ACA territory on the medial surface). Left neglect (spatial inattention) is a non-dominant (right hemisphere) parietal sign. The combination of contralateral face/arm > leg weakness, sensory loss, and neglect is classic for right MCA territory ischaemic stroke.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Right PCA territory stroke causes a contralateral homonymous hemianopia, memory disturbance, and alexia without agraphia — not predominantly motor/sensory loss with neglect.
Right MCA territory is the correct answer: contralateral (left) face and arm > leg weakness, hemisensory loss, and left-sided neglect.
Left MCA stroke would cause right-sided deficits and, in a right-handed patient, aphasia — not the picture described.
Right ACA territory causes predominantly contralateral leg weakness with less arm and face involvement — opposite of the pattern here.
Basilar artery territory strokes cause bilateral signs, locked-in syndrome, cranial nerve palsies, and altered consciousness — not a unilateral hemisyndrome.
clinicalPearl: In MCA territory stroke: face and arm affected more than leg; neglect indicates the non-dominant (right) hemisphere; aphasia indicates the dominant (left) hemisphere.
keyClue: Left face and arm > leg weakness with left neglect in a right-handed man — points to right hemisphere (non-dominant) MCA territory
commonExamTrap: Confusing the side of the lesion with the side of symptoms — the stroke is in the RIGHT hemisphere but the deficits are on the LEFT.
missedConcept: Hemispatial neglect is a non-dominant (right hemisphere) parietal feature and strongly localises the stroke to the right MCA territory.
redFlagReason: Acute ischaemic stroke is a time-critical emergency; thrombolysis within 4.5 hours and thrombectomy within 24 hours can dramatically reduce disability.

clinicalStem: A 72-year-old woman is brought to the emergency department with acute onset right arm weakness and expressive dysphasia starting 90 minutes ago. Vital signs: BP 172/98 mmHg, HR 88 bpm regular, O2 sat 98% on air. Fingerprick glucose is 5.8 mmol/L. She has no contraindications to thrombolysis. What is the SINGLE MOST IMPORTANT immediate investigation before administering IV alteplase?
explanation: Before administering IV thrombolysis for suspected ischaemic stroke, the single most important investigation is a non-contrast CT head. This is to exclude intracerebral haemorrhage, which is the most critical contraindication to thrombolysis. CT is fast (minutes), widely available, and excellent at identifying haemorrhage. If CT shows no bleed, thrombolysis can proceed. MRI DWI is more sensitive for early ischaemia but takes longer and is not required before thrombolysis when clinical diagnosis is clear.
wrongAnswerExplanations: MRI with DWI is more sensitive for detecting early ischaemia but is slower; it is not required prior to thrombolysis when the primary purpose is to exclude haemorrhage.
Non-contrast CT head is correct — it rapidly excludes haemorrhage and is the mandatory pre-thrombolysis investigation.
CT perfusion is useful for selecting patients for thrombectomy in late-window presentations but is not required before thrombolysis in a clear early-window case.
Carotid Doppler is important for secondary prevention workup but is not needed before thrombolysis and should not cause delay.
ECG and troponin are important to detect AF and concurrent ACS but do not affect the decision to give thrombolysis in this window and should not delay CT.
clinicalPearl: Non-contrast CT head is mandatory before IV thrombolysis to exclude haemorrhage — its main role in hyperacute stroke is ruling out bleed, not confirming ischaemia.
keyClue: Acute focal deficit within 90 minutes + no contraindications + thrombolysis being considered
commonExamTrap: Ordering MRI over CT — MRI is better for ischaemia detection but delay risks missing the thrombolysis window; CT is faster and sufficient.
missedConcept: CT sensitivity for haemorrhage (nearly 100%) is what matters before thrombolysis, not sensitivity for ischaemia.
redFlagReason: Administering thrombolysis in haemorrhagic stroke is catastrophic; CT to exclude bleed is a non-negotiable step before alteplase.

clinicalStem: A 65-year-old man is admitted with a left MCA ischaemic stroke confirmed on MRI DWI. His routine ECG on admission shows sinus rhythm. BP 148/88 mmHg, LDL 3.4 mmol/L. There is no carotid bruit. Carotid Doppler shows less than 30% stenosis bilaterally. An echocardiogram is normal. After 5 days of continuous cardiac monitoring, no arrhythmia is detected. Which investigation would MOST likely identify the underlying embolic source if it is still undetected?
options: Prolonged ambulatory cardiac monitoring for 30 days
explanation: Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (PAF) is the most common undetected embolic source in cryptogenic ischaemic stroke. Short-term monitoring (24–48 hour Holter or even 5 days of continuous in-hospital monitoring) misses PAF in a large proportion of patients. Prolonged ambulatory monitoring over 30 days (implantable loop recorder or external patch monitor) detects PAF in up to 25–30% of cryptogenic stroke patients, directly changing management (anticoagulation rather than antiplatelet therapy). This is now guideline-recommended after a negative short-term workup.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Repeat MRI with contrast defines the extent of ischaemia and may show leptomeningeal enhancement but does not identify the embolic source.

Transcranial Doppler bubble study (with agitated saline) detects a patent foramen ovale (PFO) and is appropriate in younger patients with cryptogenic stroke, but PAF is a more common detectable cause in a 65-year-old.
Prolonged 30-day ambulatory cardiac monitoring is the correct next step to detect paroxysmal AF — the highest-yield investigation in this age group with cryptogenic stroke.
Antiphospholipid antibodies are relevant in younger patients or those with recurrent thrombosis/miscarriage; less likely to be the primary cause in a 65-year-old man.
CT pulmonary angiography is for suspected pulmonary embolism; there is no indication for this in an ischaemic stroke workup.
clinicalPearl: Paroxysmal AF is found in up to 25–30% of cryptogenic stroke patients on prolonged 30-day monitoring — always extend monitoring beyond routine Holter after an unrevealing initial workup.
keyClue: Cryptogenic ischaemic stroke in a 65-year-old + normal short-term cardiac monitoring + normal carotid and echo
commonExamTrap: Assuming normal 24-hour Holter or 5-day in-hospital monitoring excludes AF — paroxysmal AF is intermittent by definition.
missedConcept: Prolonged ambulatory cardiac monitoring (30 days) is superior to short-term monitoring for detecting paroxysmal AF in cryptogenic stroke.

clinicalStem: A 55-year-old hypertensive man presents with sudden onset vertigo, vomiting, difficulty swallowing, and hoarseness. On examination: right-sided ptosis and miosis, loss of pain and temperature sensation over the right face and left arm and leg, and right-sided limb ataxia with a tendency to fall to the right. There is no limb weakness. Which artery is MOST likely occluded?
explanation: This is Wallenberg's syndrome (lateral medullary syndrome), caused by occlusion of the posterior inferior cerebellar artery (PICA) or its parent vessel (the vertebral artery). The classic features are: ipsilateral Horner's syndrome (ptosis, miosis — right), ipsilateral facial pain/temperature loss (right), contralateral body pain/temperature loss (left arm/leg — spinothalamic tract dissociation), ipsilateral limb ataxia (right), dysphagia, hoarseness (nucleus ambiguus), and vertigo. Crucially, there is no motor weakness because the corticospinal tract is spared in the lateral medulla.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Left MCA occlusion causes contralateral (right) face, arm, and leg weakness with possible aphasia — no Horner's syndrome, ataxia, or crossed sensory pattern.
Proximal basilar artery occlusion causes bilateral long-tract signs, cranial nerve palsies, altered consciousness, or locked-in syndrome — not the unilateral lateral medullary picture.
Right PICA occlusion causes Wallenberg's syndrome — this is the correct answer.

Right AICA infarction causes ipsilateral facial numbness, ipsilateral deafness/tinnitus (cochlear involvement), and ipsilateral Horner's, but the hearing loss and the facial nerve palsy distinguish AICA from PICA.
Left PCA occlusion causes right homonymous hemianopia and possibly alexia, memory disturbance — not a lateral medullary syndrome.
clinicalPearl: Wallenberg's syndrome (PICA/vertebral occlusion): ipsilateral Horner's + ipsilateral facial sensory loss + contralateral body sensory loss + ipsilateral ataxia — with NO weakness.
keyClue: Crossed sensory loss (ipsilateral face vs contralateral body) + ipsilateral Horner's + ipsilateral ataxia + dysphagia/hoarseness + NO motor weakness
commonExamTrap: Confusing PICA (Wallenberg's) with AICA syndrome — both cause ipsilateral Horner's and ataxia, but AICA additionally causes ipsilateral deafness and facial nerve palsy.
missedConcept: Lateral medullary syndrome spares the corticospinal tract (pure sensory and cerebellar signs); the crossed sensory pattern is the hallmark.

subtopic: CT head indications in mild TBI
clinicalStem: A 30-year-old motorcyclist is brought to the emergency department after a road traffic collision. He was not wearing a helmet. He was briefly confused at the scene (approximately 3 minutes) but GCS is now 15. He has no focal neurological deficits. He reports post-traumatic amnesia for approximately 20 minutes. He vomited once in the ambulance. His head CT is not yet done. According to NICE head injury guidelines (NICE CG176), which finding ALONE would be an indication for an IMMEDIATE CT head?
options: GCS of 15 with post-traumatic amnesia > 30 minutes
Brief loss of consciousness lasting under 5 minutes
GCS 14 at any time since the injury
explanation: NICE CG176 specifies that a GCS score of < 15 at any time since the injury is an indication for immediate CT head in adults. A GCS of 14 at any time (even if it has since returned to 15) is a standalone indication. Post-traumatic amnesia > 30 minutes, two or more episodes of vomiting, age > 65 with any loss of consciousness, coagulopathy, or suspected skull fracture are also indications, but here only one episode of vomiting occurred and GCS is currently 15 — the single item from the options that is an immediate standalone NICE indication is GCS 14 at any time.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Post-traumatic amnesia > 30 minutes is a NICE indication; however, in this scenario the amnesia is described as approximately 20 minutes — not > 30 minutes.
A single episode of vomiting is not a standalone NICE indication; two or more episodes of vomiting are required.
Brief loss of consciousness under 5 minutes alone is not listed as a standalone NICE indication for immediate CT in the absence of other risk factors.
GCS 14 at any time since injury is a standalone NICE indication for immediate CT head — this is the correct answer.
Headache alone without other high-risk features is not a standalone indication for immediate CT under NICE guidelines.
clinicalPearl: Any GCS < 15 (i.e., GCS 14) at any time since head injury — even briefly — is a NICE standalone indication for immediate CT head.
keyClue: GCS 14 at any time since the injury
commonExamTrap: Assuming the current GCS of 15 negates earlier reduced consciousness — NICE bases the indication on the worst GCS since injury, not on arrival GCS.
missedConcept: NICE CG176 head CT criteria apply to the GCS nadir since injury; a transient drop to 14 triggers CT even if the patient recovers to 15.

clinicalStem: A 19-year-old male is brought unconscious to the emergency department after being struck on the left side of the head with a cricket bat. Initial paramedic assessment found him alert and talking at the scene (GCS 15). On arrival 45 minutes later his GCS has dropped to 9, he has a fixed dilated right pupil, and left-sided weakness. CT head shows a biconvex (lenticular) hyperdense lesion over the left temporal region with significant midline shift. Which diagnosis BEST explains these findings?
explanation: The classic presentation of extradural haematoma (EDH) is: temporal impact (often involving the pterion — the thinnest point of the skull), a lucid interval (alert at scene), then rapid deterioration as the haematoma expands. The fixed dilated ipsilateral (right) pupil indicates uncal herniation with compression of CN III on the right side as the left-sided haematoma pushes the brainstem across. Left temporal location causes right-sided CN III compression. CT shows the classic biconvex (lenticular) hyperdense lesion bounded by dural attachments at suture lines — characteristic of EDH from middle meningeal artery rupture.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Acute subdural haematoma is caused by bridging vein rupture and appears as a crescent-shaped hyperdensity that crosses suture lines — not biconvex; it is more common in elderly patients on anticoagulants.

Diffuse axonal injury (DAI) typically follows acceleration-deceleration injury, causes loss of consciousness from the moment of impact (no lucid interval), and shows multiple small haemorrhages at grey-white junctions on MRI — not a focal biconvex lesion.
Left temporal extradural haematoma is correct — biconvex lesion + lucid interval + temporal impact + rapid deterioration + uncal herniation.
Hypertensive intracerebral haemorrhage occurs in hypertensive older adults, is typically deep (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons) and irregular in shape — not a biconvex extra-axial lesion.
Cerebral contusion appears as a heterogeneous area of mixed density within the brain parenchyma, not a biconvex extra-axial hyperdensity.
clinicalPearl: Extradural haematoma: lucid interval after temporal head trauma + biconvex CT lesion + ipsilateral CN III palsy (blown pupil) = neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate evacuation.
keyClue: Lucid interval after temporal impact + biconvex (lenticular) hyperdense CT lesion
commonExamTrap: Confusing the dilated pupil with the side of the haematoma — the blown pupil is ipsilateral to the haematoma (herniation compresses CN III on the same side), not contralateral.
missedConcept: The lucid interval is pathognomonic of EDH: arterial haematoma (middle meningeal artery) takes time to expand before causing herniation.
redFlagReason: Extradural haematoma with uncal herniation (fixed dilated pupil, falling GCS) is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate CT and craniotomy; delay causes death or permanent disability.
